cf_parsed.updated_plan_state: Baseline 197 has been revalidated end-to-end. No verified improving coupled schedule was found; keep the valid incumbent rather than risk an invalid replacement.

parsed.subtask_body: I tested the “improved A releases + rebuilt B” direction more carefully and found the earlier A candidate is itself infeasible on machine overlaps, so it cannot be used as a basis.
parsed.updated_plan_state: The alternative A schedule explored earlier is not trustworthy enough to build on. The safest path is either stop with the valid 197 baseline or spend one last short pass deriving stronger lower bounds/critical-path arguments and a tiny neighborhood search around baseline late operations, replacing only if a complete explicit improvement is verified.

transcript.content:
PLAN_STATE: Use the baseline as safety. First estimate strong lower bounds from total loads and job-chain lengths in both factories to see available improvement margin. Then hand-construct a better schedule by focusing on Factory A release times into B and the heaviest/critical B machines. Prioritize reducing late A completions for jobs with long B tails, while keeping A machine conflicts feasible. After that, sequence Factory B greedily by job release times and machine bottlenecks, validating all precedences exactly. If a clearly improved full feasible schedule emerges, emit it; otherwise preserve baseline-valid structure and only stop with a candidate I can fully verify. Reserve time for one refinement pass on the critical path and possible local swaps on bottleneck machines. Keep all emitted schedules explicit machine-by-machine with every operation exactly once.
NEXT_SUB: {"id": 1, "desc": "Compute lower bounds and identify critical jobs/machines; then build one explicit improved feasible coupled schedule from scratch or by targeted modification of the baseline", "p_solve": 0.42, "time_budget_s": 240}

SUB_1: Lower bounds: Factory A total load 147 on MA4 and chain max about 42; Factory B total load only 34 max on MB1/MB5, so coupled optimum is driven mainly by A plus one job’s B tail.
SUB_1: A job totals: J1 A25/B30, J2 A42/B33, J3 A40/B24, J4 A35/B30, J5 A19/B35, J6 A30/B38. Baseline A completions are [25,61,96,121,130,148].
SUB_1: Since J6 has the longest B tail (38), baseline 148+38=186 already hints the 197 schedule has sizable avoidable slack, mostly in B waiting and late A ordering.
SUB_1: I looked for a compact constructive improvement by front-loading short/critical A chains rather than the baseline’s very delayed J4/J5/J6.
SUB_1: A feasible early-start Factory A schedule exists with completions:
SUB_1: J5=19, J1=32, J4=39, J6=42, J3=51, J2=58.
SUB_1: One explicit feasible A machine schedule:
SUB_1: MA1 J3[0,5] J5[5,6] J1[8,10] J4[18,23] J6[32,33] J2[46,53]
SUB_1: MA2 J1[0,5] J4[6,9] J5[9,12] J6[16,24] J3[38,47] J2[53,58]
SUB_1: MA3 J5[0,1] J4[1,7] J1[5,8] J2[14,23] J3[24,31] J6[33,34]
SUB_1: MA4 J5[6,7] J1[10,15] J6[7,11] J4[16,18] J3[18,24] J2[23,27]
SUB_1: MA5 J5[12,15] J2[5,10] J4[23,27] J1[19,20] J3[31,37] J6[34,39]
SUB_1: MA6 J6[24,28] J5[19,28] J4[31,39] J3[47,51] J2[39,46] J1[27,32]
SUB_1: MA7 J2[0,5] J3[5,8] J4[9,16] J6[16,23] J1[15,19] J5[15,16]
SUB_1: I then built a feasible B schedule from releases r=(32,58,51,39,19,42), but the first hand-built attempt gave makespan 201, worse than baseline; B conflicts dominate if sequenced naively.
SUB_1: So I am not replacing the incumbent without a fully verified better complete coupled schedule.
SUB_1: Main insight for next turn: use the much better A releases above, then optimize B around bottlenecks MB1/MB5 and the long J6/J5 tails; there is realistic room below 197 if B is packed well.
SUB_1: Current safe best remains the provided valid baseline 197.

UPDATED_PLAN_STATE: Keep the baseline as the incumbent. Next, use the improved Factory A release schedule already identified and spend one focused pass constructing a tightly packed Factory B schedule, especially around MB1 and MB5, only replacing the incumbent if the full coupled schedule is explicitly verified and beats 197.
QUALITY_FORECAST: {"p_gap_le_2":0.02,"p_gap_le_5":0.12,"p_gap_le_10":0.4}
CONTINUE_FORECAST: {"p_improve":0.4,"expected_delta_score":3.0,"expected_gap_reduction":5.0}
DECISION: continue
NEXT_SUB: {"id":2,"desc":"Starting from the improved Factory A releases (J5=19,J1=32,J4=39,J6=42,J3=51,J2=58), hand-optimize and fully verify a Factory B schedule; emit only if complete makespan beats 197","p_solve":0.4,"time_budget_s":180}

SUB_2: I tested the “improved A releases + rebuilt B” direction more carefully and found the earlier A candidate is itself infeasible on machine overlaps, so it cannot be used as a basis.
SUB_2: Specifically, in that candidate A schedule MA4 had J5[6,7] overlapping J6[7,11] okay, but MA5 had J2[5,10] before J2 could reach MA5 (needs MA7 then MA5, earliest 5) okay; the deeper issue is MA4 order vs job precedences and MA6/J1 timing consistency, making the stated completion vector unreliable. I therefore discarded it rather than risk emitting an invalid full schedule.
SUB_2: I then checked targeted baseline modifications on the critical late jobs J5/J6 in Factory B. J6’s B chain is tightly gated by MB1 then MB2 then MB6 then MB3 then MB5; baseline already keeps these nearly contiguous from 159 onward except for unavoidable precedence waits.
SUB_2: For J5, moving earlier in B would require earlier access to MB5/MB1/MB4/MB2/MB7/MB6/MB3, but those machines are occupied by predecessor-constrained chains from J4 and J6. Simple swaps either violate job order or delay J6 enough to erase gains.
SUB_2: I also checked whether any single-machine reorder in baseline A could substantially advance J6 completion without pushing another longer B-tail job later. Because J6 needs MA7→MA4→MA2→MA6→MA1→MA3→MA5 and several of those machines are already occupied by jobs whose own downstream chains are critical, obvious pulls-forward create cascaded delays.
SUB_2: Net result: no fully verified schedule better than 197 was constructed this turn.
SUB_2: Safe incumbent remains the provided baseline, which is fully explicit and valid.

CONTINUE_FORECAST: {"p_improve":0.12,"expected_delta_score":0.8,"expected_gap_reduction":1.5}
DECISION: stop

SUB_3: I used the forced extra turn only for a final sanity check on whether the baseline 197 schedule might hide an obvious 1-machine improvement.
SUB_3: Factory A is valid as given: each job follows its A route exactly, and the A completion times are J1=25, J2=61, J3=96, J4=121, J5=130, J6=148.
SUB_3: On Factory B, all jobs also follow their B routes exactly and each starts no earlier than its A completion.
SUB_3: The baseline B completion times are J1=55, J2=94, J3=120, J4=151, J5=176, J6=197.
SUB_3: The terminal critical tail is J6 on B:
SUB_3: MB4 159-164 -> MB7 164-168 -> MB1 168-177 -> MB2 177-181 -> MB6 181-185 -> MB3 185-192 -> MB5 192-197.
SUB_3: This suffix is fully contiguous except for zero slack handoffs, so reducing makespan needs J6 to enter MB4 earlier than 159 or reach MB5 earlier than 192.
SUB_3: But J6 cannot reach MB4 before completing A at 148 plus its first B op MB4(5), so 153 is the absolute earliest second-step release to MB7 and 157 the earliest MB1 release even in an idealized B packing.
SUB_3: I checked the actual blockers near that tail: MB1 has J5 149-153 and then idle until J6 168; MB7 has J5 160-162 before J6 164-168; MB6 has J5 162-171 before J6 181-185; MB3 has J5 171-176 before J6 185-192; MB5 has J5 141-149 before J6 192-197.
SUB_3: Those observations mean local swaps around only one of MB1/MB7/MB6/MB3/MB5 do not unlock a shorter J6 finish; the bigger limiter is J6’s late A completion plus its own long chained B route.
SUB_3: Since no complete better explicit schedule was constructed and the incumbent is fully validated, the best safe answer remains the baseline makespan 197.
